clips.title: 穿着巧克力衬衫的70岁老人

source.episode: NPR

lines.en: Brad is 70, retired in West Palm Beach, Florida.
lines.zh: 布拉德今年70岁，退休后住在佛罗里达州西棕榈滩。

lines.en: And when we first talked to him, he showed up wearing a bright orange, I guess, Hawaiian shirt, but with a bunch of Reese's peanut butter cups all over it.
lines.zh: 我们第一次和他聊的时候，他穿着一件亮橙色的——我猜算是夏威夷衫吧——上面还印满了Reese's花生酱杯。

lines.en: Honestly, pretty snazzy.
lines.zh: 说实话，还挺拉风的。

lines.en: I mean, I guess you could call me a big Reese's fan.
lines.zh: 我的意思是，你也可以说我是个超级Reese's粉丝。

lines.en: And it's his love of Reese's that is keeping him from his beloved karaoke right now.
lines.zh: 而正是他对Reese's的热爱，让他现在没法去唱他最爱的卡拉OK。

lines.en: Yeah, because a little while ago, he heard that the Hershey Company had released a new Reese's chocolate.
lines.zh: 对，因为前阵子他听说好时公司推出了一款新的Reese's巧克力。

lines.en: It was Reese's peanut butter mini hearts unwrapped.
lines.zh: 那款产品叫Reese's迷你花生酱爱心巧克力（独立无包装版）。

lines.en: OK, and of course, then I went out and bought one, bought a pouch.
lines.zh: 然后我当然就跑去买了一包。

lines.en: Brad opens the bag and pops some mini hearts in his mouth.
lines.zh: 布拉德打开袋子，往嘴里丢了几颗迷你爱心巧克力。

lines.en: And I took two bites and it was not recognizable.
lines.zh: 我咬了两口，就发现这味道完全不对。

lines.en: It was just nasty.
lines.zh: 那东西简直难吃死了。

lines.en: It was not edible.
lines.zh: 根本没法吃。

lines.en: Brad spits out the mini hearts and then he dumps them in the trash.
lines.zh: 布拉德把那些迷你爱心巧克力吐了出来，然后直接扔进了垃圾桶。

lines.en: But then he's like, wait, what was so off about those chocolate hearts?
lines.zh: 但接着他又说，等等，那些巧克力爱心到底哪里不对劲？

lines.en: I retrieved the pouch wrapper and looked carefully at the front and the back.
lines.zh: 我把那个小袋子的包装捡了回来，仔细看了看正面和背面。

lines.en: And there was no milk chocolate and there was no real peanut butter.
lines.zh: 结果里面既没有牛奶巧克力，也没有真正的花生酱。

lines.en: So it wasn't even, I don't know what I, Greg, I have no idea what I was eating or what I was tasting.
lines.zh: 所以那甚至都不算——我都不知道该怎么说，Greg，我完全不知道自己刚才吃的、尝到的到底是什么。

lines.en: Brad is like, what is even in here?
lines.zh: 布拉德就说，这里面到底装的是什么啊？

lines.en: No milk chocolate, no peanut butter in a chocolate Reese's peanut butter cop light product?
lines.zh: 一个仿瑞斯花生酱杯的巧克力产品，居然没有牛奶巧克力，也没有花生酱？

clips.title: 她用鼻子诊断了一种病

lines.en: Les and I met when we were 16 at school, and he had this wonderful male musk smell.
lines.zh: 我和莱斯16岁时在学校相识，他身上有一种很好闻的男性麝香气息。

lines.en: He knew I was sensitive, so he didn't wear any perfume or deodorant.
lines.zh: 他知道我很敏感，所以不用香水，也不喷除臭剂。

lines.en: However, when he was 31, that smell began to change, and it worried me.
lines.zh: 然而，在他31岁时，那种气味开始发生变化，这让我很担心。

lines.en: By the time he was 33, this smell was his dominant smell.
lines.zh: 到他33岁时，这种气味已经成了他身上最明显的味道。

lines.en: But unknown to us, I had diagnosed him with a disease.
lines.zh: 但我们当时并不知道，我其实已经诊断出了他得了病。

lines.en: My nose had diagnosed him.
lines.zh: 准确地说，是我的鼻子诊断出了他的病。

lines.en: And it took medicine 12 years later to catch up, and he was diagnosed with Parkinson's disease.
lines.zh: 直到12年后，医学才终于跟上这一发现，他被确诊患上了帕金森病。

lines.en: Born with a hypersensitive nose, Joy began to recognize diseases through their scents during her decades of work as a nurse.
lines.zh: 乔伊天生嗅觉异常敏锐，在数十年的护士生涯中，她开始通过气味识别疾病。

lines.en: She shares the journey she's been on to make the best of her super smelling abilities,.
lines.zh: 她分享了自己一路走来的经历，讲述她如何尽可能发挥自己超强嗅觉的优势。

lines.en: And how her extraordinary nose has been translated into a non-invasive test, helping researchers diagnose what was right under their noses all along.
lines.zh: 以及她那非凡的嗅觉如何被转化为一种无创检测方法，帮助研究人员诊断出那个其实一直近在眼前的问题。

lines.en: That's coming up right after a short break.
lines.zh: 短暂休息之后，马上回来。

clips.title: 第一支烟和最后一支烟

source.episode: Resolutions

lines.en: I remember my first cigarette almost as well as I remember my last.
lines.zh: 我记得我的第一支烟，几乎就像记得最后一支那样清楚。

lines.en: My first one, it was all love and honey.
lines.zh: 第一支的时候，一切都像爱情和蜜糖一样甜美。

lines.en: It tasted good.
lines.zh: 它尝起来真不错。

lines.en: It felt good on my lips.
lines.zh: 它贴在我唇边的感觉也很好。

lines.en: It melted my brain like chocolate under a hot marshmallow.
lines.zh: 它像热棉花糖底下的巧克力一样，把我的脑子都融化了。

lines.en: Last one, not so much.
lines.zh: 可最后一支，就完全不是那么回事了。

lines.en: It was one of those random summertime hailstorms, and I was huddled underneath this little overhang, smoking it with abandon, desperate need.
lines.zh: 那是夏天里一场突如其来的冰雹雨，我蜷缩在一个小小的屋檐下，放纵地抽着那支烟，带着一种近乎绝望的渴求。

lines.en: I smoked it all the way down to the filter, flicked it into this little flowerpot that, poor little thing, never saw anything beautiful.
lines.zh: 我一直抽到只剩过滤嘴，才把它弹进旁边那个小花盆里——可怜的小东西，怕是从没见过什么美好的东西。

lines.en: And I felt so frustrated with myself, so ashamed.
lines.zh: 那一刻，我对自己无比懊恼，也无比羞愧。

lines.en: I'd tried to quit plenty of times before, and there I was again.
lines.zh: 以前我已经试过很多次戒烟了，可到头来，我还是又回到了原点。

lines.en: I felt like I was letting myself down.
lines.zh: 我觉得自己辜负了自己。

lines.en: I felt like I was breaking a promise, which I was.
lines.zh: 我感觉自己违背了一个承诺，而事实也的确如此。

lines.en: I went back inside, and I went into my bedroom where I've got this big whiteboard.
lines.zh: 我回到屋里，走进了我的卧室，那里有一块很大的白板。

lines.en: It was my quitboard, and it was huge, and I only had like 60 little tallies in a corner.
lines.zh: 那是我用来记录戒掉进度的白板，很大一块，可角落里却只画了大概60个小小的记号。

clips.title: 被债务淹没的体面人生

lines.en: It happens all the time.
lines.zh: 这种事一直都在发生。

lines.en: The lovely couple with a nice house, beautiful family and gainful careers unexpectedly find themselves drowning in tens of thousands of dollars of credit card debt.
lines.zh: 那对看起来很幸福的夫妻，住着不错的房子，家庭美满，事业也稳定，却突然发现自己陷入了数万美元的信用卡债务之中。

lines.en: Debt grew by 93 billion dollars at the end of 2024 and half of that increase came from new credit card debt.
lines.zh: 到2024年底，债务增加了930亿美元，其中一半的增幅来自新增的信用卡债务。

lines.en: Now New York Federal Reserve.
lines.zh: 这是纽约联邦储备银行的数据。

lines.en: Or maybe it's the fresh college graduate who, rather than looking forward to a bright future, finds himself trying to pay back a series of compounding loans.
lines.zh: 又或者是那个刚毕业的大学生，本该期待光明的未来，却发现自己正在偿还一连串不断累积的贷款。

lines.en: Only to watch the balance grow year after year after year.
lines.zh: 只能眼睁睁看着余额一年又一年地不断增长。

lines.en: One out of every four Americans with student loans is delinquent.
lines.zh: 每四个背负学生贷款的美国人中，就有一个处于拖欠状态。

lines.en: That's nearly triple the pre-pandemic delinquency rates.
lines.zh: 这几乎是疫情前拖欠率的三倍。

lines.en: And then there's the retiree who thought she had enough saved until one medical emergency wiped out her nest egg.
lines.zh: 还有那些退休人士，本以为自己的积蓄已经足够，结果一场突如其来的医疗紧急情况就把养老钱掏空了。

lines.en: And so you just start chipping away at your savings, chipping away at everything you have and ultimately chipping away at retirement.
lines.zh: 于是你只能一点点动用积蓄，一点点耗掉自己拥有的一切，最终连退休生活也被一点点蚕食。

lines.en: It's easy to believe this would never happen to me.
lines.zh: 人很容易觉得，这种事绝不会发生在自己身上。

lines.en: I would be smarter, savvier, better at saving.
lines.zh: 我本来可以更精明、更老练，也更会存钱。

lines.en: And yet hundreds of millions of people around the world find themselves enmeshed in debt.
lines.zh: 然而，世界各地仍有数以亿计的人深陷债务之中。

lines.en: Some of this has to do with reckless spending of course.
lines.zh: 当然，这其中一部分原因确实与不计后果的消费有关。

lines.en: But even when you do everything right, budget, plan and make reasonable choices,.
lines.zh: 但即使你把该做的都做对了，认真做预算、提前规划，并作出合理的选择，

clips.title: 他咬了一口，吐了出来

source.episode: NPR

lines.en: He heard that the Hershey Company had released a new Reese's chocolate.
lines.zh: 他听说好时公司推出了一款新的 Reese's 巧克力。

lines.en: It was Reese's peanut butter mini hearts unwrapped.
lines.zh: 那是一款没独立包装的 Reese's 迷你花生酱爱心巧克力。

lines.en: OK, and of course, then I went out and bought one, bought a pouch.
lines.zh: 好吧，结果我当然还是跑去买了，买了一袋。

lines.en: Brad opens the bag and pops some mini hearts in his mouth.
lines.zh: 布拉德打开袋子，往嘴里丢了几颗迷你爱心巧克力。

lines.en: And I took two bites and it was not recognizable.

lines.en: It was not edible.

lines.en: Brad spits out the mini hearts and then he dumps them in the trash.
lines.zh: 布拉德把迷你爱心巧克力吐了出来，然后全扔进了垃圾桶。

lines.en: But then he's like, wait, what was so off about those chocolate hearts?
lines.zh: 但接着他又想，等等，这些巧克力爱心到底哪里不对劲？

lines.en: I retrieved the pouch wrapper and looked carefully at the front and the back.

lines.zh: 所以那东西根本算不上什么，我都不知道自己吃的到底是什么、尝到的又是什么，Greg，我完全搞不清。

lines.en: Brad is like, what is even in here?
lines.zh: Brad 就说，这里面到底装的是什么啊？

lines.en: No milk chocolate, no peanut butter in a chocolate Reese's peanut butter cop light product?
lines.zh: 一个号称是 Reese's 花生酱巧克力杯的产品，居然没有牛奶巧克力，也没有花生酱？

lines.en: I went to the store and just investigated everything I could.

clips.title: 1928年奥运会，女性第一次站上跑道

source.episode: Tested (Ep 2): Questions of a Physical Nature

lines.en: Let's begin in the summer of 1928.
lines.zh: 让我们从1928年的夏天说起。

lines.en: Almost a hundred years ago now.
lines.zh: 那已经是将近一百年前的事了。

lines.en: That August, the Olympics was held in Amsterdam, and almost every day the Olympic Stadium was packed with fans.
lines.zh: 那年8月，奥运会在阿姆斯特丹举行，几乎每天奥林匹克体育场都挤满了观众。

lines.en: And those fans were watching something historic.
lines.zh: 而这些观众正在见证一项历史性时刻。

lines.en: This was the first Olympics where women were allowed to compete in track and field.
lines.zh: 这是奥运会历史上第一次允许女性参加田径比赛。

lines.en: Women had been allowed in the Olympics before 1928.
lines.zh: 其实在1928年之前，女性就已经被允许参加奥运会了。

lines.en: They could play tennis or swim, sports that were considered delicate and feminine.
lines.zh: 她们可以打网球或游泳，这些项目被认为更轻柔、更符合女性气质。

lines.en: But track and field had always been completely off limits.
lines.zh: 但田径项目一直是完全禁止女性参加的。

lines.en: And on August 2nd, a particularly exciting race happened.
lines.zh: 而在8月2日这天，一场格外精彩的比赛上演了。

lines.en: The 800 meters. Two laps.
lines.zh: 女子800米。两圈。

lines.en: Half a mile.
lines.zh: 半英里。

lines.en: This was the longest distance women were allowed to run.
lines.zh: 这是当时女性被允许参加跑步的最长距离。

lines.en: There is some silent footage from the race that still survives.
lines.zh: 那场比赛的一些无声影像至今仍然保存了下来。

lines.en: And as the camera pans across the crowd, you can see fans cheering, waving hats, and leaning over.
lines.zh: 当镜头扫过人群时，你可以看到观众在欢呼、挥舞帽子，还纷纷探身向前。

clips.title: 波本酒局内幕

source.episode: Bourbon Economics

lines.en: Okay, this show has economics in its name, so let's start with an economist.
lines.zh: 好，这档节目名字里有“经济学”，那我们就先从一位经济学家说起。

lines.en: My name is Ken Trosky.
lines.zh: 我叫肯·特罗斯基。

lines.en: I'm a labor economist and chair of the economics department at the University of Kentucky.
lines.zh: 我是劳动经济学家，也是肯塔基大学经济系主任。

lines.en: Trosky grew up in Washington state, got his PhD at the University of Chicago,.
lines.zh: 特罗斯基在华盛顿州长大，在芝加哥大学拿到博士学位，

lines.en: And he moved to Kentucky about 20 years ago. 95 of bourbon made in the world is made within a 45 minute drive of the house I live in.
lines.zh: 大约20年前搬到了肯塔基。我家方圆45分钟车程内，生产了全世界95%的波本威士忌。

lines.en: It was Trosky we heard from a minute ago saying that there are 16 million barrels of bourbon aging in Kentucky.
lines.zh: 刚才我们听到的那位特罗斯基还提到，肯塔基州有1600万桶波本威士忌正在陈酿。

lines.en: That is up from 4 million when Trosky arrived.
lines.zh: 而在特罗斯基刚到那里的时候，这个数字只有400万桶。

lines.en: When I got there, bourbon was just starting to recover from a long period of time of when it was in decline.
lines.zh: 我刚到那儿时，波本威士忌正开始从长期低迷中慢慢恢复。

lines.en: And I was hearing about this bourbon called Pappy Van Winkles.
lines.zh: 那时候我一直听人提起一种叫帕比·范温克尔的波本。

lines.en: I told my wife one time for Christmas, I said, you know, I'd like to try some.
lines.zh: 有一年圣诞节，我跟我妻子说，你知道，我想尝尝那个。

lines.en: So she went into the local big box liquor store, and there were a couple of bottles of 20 year old Pappy,.
lines.zh: 于是她去了当地一家大型连锁酒类商店，看到那儿有几瓶20年陈的帕比。

lines.en: But it was 120 a bottle, which is maybe triple what a typical bottle would be.
lines.zh: 但一瓶要120美元，差不多是普通一瓶价格的三倍。

lines.en: Oh yeah, at least.
lines.zh: 哦对，至少是这样。

lines.en: OK, it's 120.
lines.zh: 好吧，120美元。

lines.en: So she thinks it's not worth it.
lines.zh: 所以她觉得不值。

lines.en: And then she goes home, and then she says, well, it's a Christmas present. Yada yada.
lines.zh: 然后她回家了，接着又说，嗯，这毕竟是圣诞礼物，诸如此类的。

lines.en: And she goes back and she buys it at 120 a bottle.
lines.zh: 然后她又回去，以120美元一瓶的价格把它买了。

lines.en: You try to find a bottle of 20 year old Pappy's right now, and it's probably twenty five hundred, three thousand.
lines.zh: 你现在去找一瓶20年陈的帕比试试，价格大概得两千五到三千美元。

lines.en: And I'm like, why didn't you get two?
lines.zh: 我当时就想，你怎么不买两瓶呢？

lines.en: Pappy Van Winkle is one of a couple dozen bourbon brands controlled by Sazerac.
lines.zh: 帕比·范·温克尔是由萨泽拉克控制的二十多个波本威士忌品牌之一。

clips.title: 11岁那年的嫉妒

source.episode: Show 859

lines.en: When Sarah was a kid, the number of movie stars who came to stay at their house was exactly one, and it was kind of a disaster.
lines.zh: 萨拉小时候，来他们家借住过的电影明星一共就只有一位，而且那次经历还挺糟糕的。

lines.en: Robert Redford ended up at their house because he had heard about a book that Sarah's stepfather was writing about Leonard Peltier of the American Indian Movement.
lines.zh: 罗伯特·雷德福会来到他们家，是因为他听说萨拉的继父正在写一本关于“美国印第安人运动”成员伦纳德·佩尔蒂埃的书。

lines.en: It was still just a manuscript, and the stepfather didn't want to send copies of it around,.
lines.zh: 那本书当时还只是手稿，继父不想把副本到处分发，

lines.en: So he told Redford that the only way that Redford could read it is if he would do it at their house in Long Island. Redford agreed.
lines.zh: 所以他告诉雷德福，如果雷德福想读，就只能到他们位于长岛的家里来看。雷德福答应了。

lines.en: Sarah says the atmosphere in the house when he arrived was completely different from any other time in her childhood.
lines.zh: 萨拉说，他到来的那天，家里的气氛和她童年里任何其他时候都完全不一样。

lines.en: I remember mostly my mother consciously trying to be very charming, and being very charming, and talking to him a lot, and asking all sorts of questions,.
lines.zh: 我记得最清楚的是，我母亲明显在刻意表现得很有魅力，而且她也确实很有魅力；她跟他说了很多话，问了各种各样的问题，

lines.en: And laughing a lot at what he said, and kind of flitting about the house in a way that I hadn't remembered her doing before.
lines.zh: 还总是对他说的话笑个不停，在屋子里轻快地来回穿梭，那样子是我以前不记得她有过的。

lines.en: When Robert Redford told stories, even the simplest story about his trip to the house,.
lines.zh: 罗伯特·雷德福讲故事的时候，哪怕只是讲一个再简单不过的、关于他来这家的路上经历的小故事，

lines.en: Her parents nodded and smiled along with an enthusiasm that the stories did not necessarily seem to merit to 11 year old Sarah.

lines.en: Most products can be sold pretty much as soon as they're made,.
lines.zh: 大多数产品几乎一生产出来就能卖掉，

lines.en: And that's important if you're in the business of making things, because you can start earning back your investment right away.

lines.en: But some products require a further investment of time after they've been manufactured.

lines.en: Certain cheeses come to mind, wine occasionally, but especially spirits.

lines.en: The most famous example is Scotch whiskey: the older the barrel, the dearer the bottle.
lines.zh: 最著名的例子就是苏格兰威士忌：酒桶陈放得越久，酒瓶就越贵。

lines.en: And the most famous American example is bourbon.
lines.zh: 而美国最著名的例子则是波本威士忌。

lines.en: I don't happen to drink much bourbon, but the bourbon industry started to sound interesting when we did a three part series on the horse industry.
lines.zh: 我自己并不怎么喝波本，但我们做了一套关于马产业的三集系列节目后，波本行业开始让我觉得很有意思。

lines.en: That series is called The Horse Is Us, if you'd like to listen.
lines.zh: 如果你想听的话，那套系列节目叫《马就是我们》。

lines.en: One interesting feature of the thoroughbred horse market is how concentrated it is in one relatively small area: The bluegrass region surrounding Lexington, Kentucky.
lines.zh: 纯血马市场一个很有意思的特点是，它高度集中在一片相对较小的区域：肯塔基州列克星敦周边的蓝草地区。

lines.en: Can you guess another industry that is concentrated in one small part of Kentucky? Correct. Bourbon.
lines.zh: 你能猜到还有哪个行业也集中在肯塔基州的一小块地方吗？没错，就是波本威士忌。

lines.en: So we thought we'd poke around that industry to see what we can learn.
lines.zh: 所以我们想去这个行业里探一探，看看能从中学到什么。

lines.en: Just one episode, though, not three.
lines.zh: 不过只做一集，不做三集。

lines.en: We will look at how time functions as an investment input.
lines.zh: 我们会看看时间是如何作为一种投资投入发挥作用的。

lines.en: And along the way, we will ask a lot of questions, like why is bourbon manufacturing so concentrated in Kentucky?
lines.zh: 在这个过程中，我们还会提出很多问题，比如，为什么波本威士忌制造业会如此集中在肯塔基州？

lines.en: They talk about the limestone, the same thing that makes fast racehorses.

lines.en: But do we also detect a note of protectionism?

lines.en: Now, let's talk about what does it even mean for content to have an impact?

lines.en: It's going to let your audience know how they should interact with you, how they then sort of categorize you in their mind and place you.

lines.zh: 以及我们到底该如何把这些真正转化为需求生成，在合适的场景、合适的渠道、面向合适的受众时，这具体应该是什么样子？

lines.en: Every piece of content matters.

lines.en: And if we're not magnetizing our contacts down the funnel and closer to a buying decision, then we're missing some opportunities and we're leaving money on the table, Christophe.

clips.title: 100年前的怀表，怎么变成今天的美国制造腕表？

source.episode: 719: Reviving the American Dream: The st

lines.en: Yeah. So Vortec watch company is like Vortex and Tik Tok, a beautiful clash of old and new.
lines.zh: 对。Vortic 这家手表公司这个名字，有点像把 Vortex 和 TikTok 结合在一起，是一种新旧交融的巧妙碰撞。

lines.en: And what we do is we actually take antique American pocket watches that were made a hundred years ago, and we turn them into one of a kind wristwatches.
lines.zh: 我们做的事情其实是把一百年前制造的美国古董怀表，改造成独一无二的腕表。

lines.en: So everything inside the watch is, you know,.
lines.zh: 所以这块表里面的所有东西，怎么说呢，

lines.en: Roughly a hundred years old and original American antique made by one of the great American watch companies like Hamilton, Elgin, Waltham or Illinois originally as a pocket watch.
lines.zh: 大约都有一百年历史了，而且都是美国原装古董，最初是由汉密尔顿、埃尔金、沃尔瑟姆或伊利诺伊等美国顶级制表公司制造的怀表。

lines.en: Fast forward to today, most pocket watches are scrapped for the gold and silver of their original case, leaving the inside as trash.
lines.zh: 到了今天，大多数怀表都会因为原表壳里的金银而被拆解报废，里面的机芯反而被当成垃圾丢掉。

lines.en: And we upcycle those, professionally restore them, meticulously rework those movements is what they're called inside the pocket watch.
lines.zh: 而我们会把这些东西升级再造，进行专业修复，并且精细地重新处理那些机芯——怀表内部的部件就是这么称呼的。

lines.en: And then we have a manufacturing business here where we're, we're making all the outside of the wristwatch,.
lines.zh: 然后我们在这里还有自己的制造业务，负责制作这款腕表外部的所有部分，

lines.en: The case, the crown, the hardware, the leather to turn that old pocket watch into a wristwatch.
lines.zh: 至于表壳、表冠、五金配件，还有把那块老怀表改成腕表所用的皮革。

lines.en: And it's one of the only truly American made wristwatches.
lines.zh: 而且这也是少数真正美国制造的腕表之一。

lines.en: It's a hundred percent made in USA built right here in Fort Collins, Colorado.
lines.zh: 百分之百美国制造，就在科罗拉多州柯林斯堡这儿生产组装。

lines.en: And it's a piece of American history on the wrist.
lines.zh: 戴在手上的，就是一段美国历史。

lines.en: So that's why I like to talk about the American dream because, you know,.
lines.zh: 所以这就是为什么我喜欢谈“美国梦”，因为你知道，

lines.en: I get to live the dream, running a manufacturing company, making American jobs and making stuff here.
lines.zh: 我正在实现这个梦想：经营一家制造企业，在美国创造就业机会，并且在这里生产产品。

lines.en: But you know, really that's what we do is we tell stories of history in these pocket watches turned into wristwatches. So interesting.
lines.zh: 但说到底，我们真正做的，是通过这些由怀表改造成的腕表来讲述历史故事。真的很有意思。

clips.title: 没人要的老怀表，为什么成了他们的宝藏？

source.episode: 719: Reviving the American Dream: The st

lines.en: Yeah. So if you've ever seen like American pickers on the History Channel, it's, it's kind of like that.
lines.zh: 对。所以如果你看过历史频道那个《American Pickers》之类的节目，大概就是那种感觉。

lines.en: You know, there's every day somewhere in the U.S.
lines.zh: 你知道，在美国几乎每天都会有地方

lines.en: A pawn shop, a jewelry store,.
lines.zh: 当铺、珠宝店之类的。

lines.en: A watchmaker goes out of business or somebody passes away.
lines.zh: 有时是钟表匠倒闭了，或者有人去世了。

lines.en: And they don't know what to do with all of the watches and diamonds and jewelry and all the things from that store.
lines.zh: 他们不知道该拿店里那些手表、钻石、珠宝以及其他东西怎么办。

lines.en: And a state buyers come in and they buy the entire state or, you know, they buy out the whole business and the diamonds go somewhere.
lines.zh: 这时候遗产收购商就会进来，把整批遗产都买下来，或者说把整个店盘下来，然后这些钻石之类的东西就会流向别处。

lines.en: The gold goes somewhere, you know, all the jewelry and rings go somewhere else.
lines.zh: 黄金会去另一个地方，你知道，其他珠宝和戒指也会流到别处。

lines.en: The watches, you know, can get sold.
lines.zh: 这些表嘛，你懂的，是能卖掉的。

lines.en: There's a huge market for vintage watches, especially Rolex and Omega and all those things.
lines.zh: 老式手表市场很大，尤其是劳力士、欧米茄这些。

lines.en: But there's almost no market for an antique American pocket watch.
lines.zh: 但古董美国产怀表基本没什么市场。

lines.en: Over 150 million pocket watches were made in USA between about 1850 and 1950.
lines.zh: 大概在1850年到1950年之间，美国一共造了超过1.5亿只怀表。

lines.en: So there's a lot of them out there.
lines.zh: 所以现在外面存量特别多。

lines.en: And most of them are sitting in a drawer in somebody's house.
lines.zh: 而且大多数都躺在别人家里的抽屉里。

lines.en: You know, it's grandpa's watch and it's just sitting there and nobody knows what to do with it.
lines.zh: 你知道，就是爷爷留下的那块表，放在那里，谁也不知道该怎么处理。

lines.en: Or it's in the bucket in the back of a pawn shop, and it's been scrapped for the gold or silver on the outside.
lines.zh: 又或者，它被扔在当铺后面桶里，外面的金或银已经被当作废料回收了。

lines.en: And they feel bad throwing it away cause they know it's a piece of history.
lines.zh: 他们又不忍心把它扔掉，因为他们知道那是一段历史的见证。

lines.en: It's a little engine that tells time that was made in Chicago a hundred years ago.
lines.zh: 这是个报时的小机器，一百年前在芝加哥造的。

lines.en: Um, and so they feel bad throwing it away, but there's really no use for it.
lines.zh: 嗯，所以他们也不忍心直接扔掉，但其实留着也没什么用。

lines.en: And so from those estate buys, you know, those estate buyers go out and they buy and they liquidate all of those stores,.
lines.zh: 所以通过这类遗产收购，你知道，那些遗产收购商会出去收货，然后把那些店里的东西统统清仓变现。

lines.en: They bring all those pocket watches and other things that are left over that people don't really have a use for to estate auctions.
lines.zh: 他们就会把那些怀表和其他剩下的、大家其实不太用得上的东西拿到遗产拍卖会上去卖。

lines.en: And I work with estate auctions to basically buy thousands of pocket watches and try to give them a second shot at life.
lines.zh: 而我会和遗产拍卖机构合作，基本上一次买下成千上万只怀表，尽量给它们第二次生命。

lines.en: We cherry pick the best ones that we can.
lines.zh: 我们会尽量把最好的那些挑出来。

lines.en: We bring them here to Fort Collins, Colorado, and my team of watchmakers goes through and restores just the best of the best of them.
lines.zh: 我们把它们带到科罗拉多州的柯林斯堡，然后由我的钟表匠团队逐一检查修复，只挑其中最好的精品来恢复。

lines.en: And then we turn them into a Vortec watch.
lines.zh: 然后我们把它们做成一块 Vortec 手表。

source.episode: Dozens of Black pilots disappeared during WWII. Who are the men still lost?

lines.en: Today, the Tuskegee Airmen are celebrated as American heroes.

lines.en: Black men who fought in World War II for a country that was still brutally segregated.

lines.en: would be awarded some of the nation's highest honors.
lines.zh: 获得了国家最高的荣誉之一。

lines.en: In 2007, the Tuskegee Airmen were awarded the Congressional Gold Medal
lines.zh: 2007年，塔斯基吉飞行员被授予国会金质奖章

lines.en: by President George W.
lines.zh: 由乔治·W总统颁发

lines.en: Bush.
lines.zh: 布什。

lines.zh: 塔斯基吉飞行员不仅帮美国赢得了战争，也让国家变得更好。

lines.en: Yours is the story of the human spirit, and it ends like all great stories do,
lines.zh: 你们的故事展现了人类精神，就像所有伟大的故事一样

lines.en: with wisdom and lessons and hope for tomorrow.
lines.zh: 以智慧、教训和对未来的希望收尾。

lines.en: But some never got to see those tributes, because they never made it home,
lines.zh: 但有些人没能看到那些致敬，因为他们没能回家。

lines.en: and their families felt they were forgotten by the U.S.

lines.en: government.

clips.title: 独立书店如何决定哪些书能上架？
clips.info_takeaway: 独立书店的书籍采购是由书店买手决定的，他们需在有限空间和薄利之间权衡，决定哪些书上架、数量多少，这直接影响书籍的商业命运。

source.episode: BOOKstore Economics

lines.en: For most of my life, whenever I walked into my local bookstore, I never gave much of a
lines.zh: 大半辈子里，每次走进我家附近的书店，我都没怎么在意

lines.en: thought to how that day's particular assortment of books actually managed to make it there.
lines.zh: 当天那些书是怎么摆到那儿的。

lines.en: I didn't suspect for a moment that for every new book on display, there might be literally
lines.zh: 我从没想到，每一本新书背后可能

lines.en: thousands of others that had been passed over and left out in the cold.
lines.zh: 有成千上万本被忽略、没人理的书。

lines.en: Until one morning this January.
lines.zh: 直到今年一月的某个早晨。

lines.en: That's when I found myself walking into a small,
lines.zh: 那天我走进一家小小的

lines.en: independent bookstore called Carmichael's in Louisville, Kentucky.
lines.zh: 位于肯塔基州路易斯维尔的独立书店，叫 Carmichael’s。

lines.en: I was there to meet a bookseller named Fisher Nash.
lines.zh: 我是去见一位叫 Fisher Nash 的书商。

lines.en: That is me.
lines.zh: 那就是我。

lines.en: I am Fisher the bookseller.
lines.zh: 我是书商 Fisher。

lines.en: Fisher greets me wearing an all green getup.
lines.zh: Fisher 穿着一身绿色来跟我打招呼。

lines.en: Green jumpsuit, socks, shoes.
lines.zh: 绿色连体衣、袜子、鞋子。

lines.en: I wore green today in honor of Planet Money.
lines.zh: 我今天也穿了绿色，向 Planet Money 致敬。

lines.en: We love that.
lines.zh: 我们喜欢这样。

lines.en: Fisher lives and breathes books.
lines.zh: Fisher 对书简直是入骨的热爱。

lines.en: If you were a character in a novel, how would you describe yourself?
lines.zh: 如果你是小说里的角色，你会怎么形容自己？

lines.en: Oh, wow.
lines.zh: 哇哦。

lines.en: That's a really hard question.
lines.zh: 这问题真难。

lines.en: I would probably say I am a quirky bookseller whose personality is mainly books.
lines.zh: 我大概会说我是个古怪的书商，性格基本就是书。

lines.en: How do you feel about You've Got Mail?
lines.zh: 你对《电子情书》有什么感觉？

lines.en: Oh, I love You've Got Mail, the Meg Ryan movie from the 90s.
lines.zh: 哦，我超喜欢《电子情书》，就是90年代梅格·瑞恩演的那部。

lines.en: It's so good.
lines.zh: 特别好看。

lines.en: It's my favorite bookstore movie.
lines.zh: 这是我最喜欢的书店题材电影。

lines.en: And in their job at Carmichael's, Fisher holds one of the greatest responsibilities
lines.zh: 在卡迈克尔书店的工作里，费舍尔肩负着最重要的职责之一

lines.en: in the whole publishing ecosystem.
lines.zh: 在整个出版圈里都是。

lines.en: Who decides what books end up in bookstores?
lines.zh: 谁来决定哪些书能进书店？

lines.en: That is me.
lines.zh: 那就是我。

lines.en: It is primarily me.
lines.zh: 主要就是我。

lines.en: I am the book buyer.
lines.zh: 我是书籍采购员。

lines.en: The book buyer is a specific role at basically every bookstore or book chain around the country.
lines.zh: 书籍采购员是全国几乎每家书店或连锁书店都会有的专门职位。

lines.en: At Carmichael's bookstore, Fisher is the book buyer.
lines.zh: 在卡迈克尔书店，费舍尔是负责进书的人。

lines.en: So I am the one who meets with sales reps from publishers, and I'm the one who decides
lines.zh: 所以我就是那个跟出版社销售代表见面的人，也是做决定的人。

lines.en: which new books we're going to bring in, how many, and where they will go.
lines.zh: 决定要进哪些新书、进多少、放在哪。

lines.en: Book buyers like Fisher will be deciding the commercial fate of the Planet Money book.
lines.zh: 像费舍尔这样的进书人会决定《Planet Money》这本书的商业命运。

lines.en: And so I wanted to understand how these decisions actually get made.
lines.zh: 所以我想弄清楚这些决定到底是怎么做的。

lines.en: Now, running an independent bookstore, Fisher explains, is a game of thin margins.
lines.zh: 费舍尔说，经营独立书店就是在薄利中求生存。

lines.en: Order too few copies of a popular new book,
lines.zh: 热门新书进货太少，

lines.en: and you'll be depriving your store of much needed financial lifeblood.
lines.zh: 就会让书店失去急需的资金来源。

lines.en: Order too many, and you'll be clogging up coveted shelf space that could be holding
lines.zh: 进货太多，就会占掉宝贵的书架空间，本来可以放别的书。

lines.zh: 休斯顿的任务控制中心发来了告别信息。

lines.en: Integrity, Houston.
lines.zh: 诚信号，休斯顿。

lines.en: You are
lines.zh: 你现在

lines.en: six minutes from your 40-minute lunar flyby
lines.zh: 距离 40 分钟的月球飞掠还有 6 分钟

lines.en: LOS.
lines.zh: 信号中断。

lines.en: From all of us, it's a privilege
lines.zh: 我们所有人都觉得很荣幸

lines.en: to witness you carrying the fire past our farthest reach.
lines.zh: 能看到你把火种带到我们最远的地方。

lines.en: Thank you.
lines.zh: 谢谢。

lines.en: unexpectedly find themselves drowning in tens of thousands of dollars of credit card debt.

lines.en: only to watch the balance grow year after year after year.

clips.title: AI其实只是数学，但它已无处不在
clips.info_takeaway: AI本质上是数学模型，通过数据学习来预测，并已深度融入我们生活的方方面面。

source.episode: Will AI destroy us... or save us?

lines.en: What does AI actually mean, you know?
lines.zh: 你知道，AI到底是什么意思？

lines.en: Really mean?
lines.zh: 真正的意思是什么？

lines.en: AI is not a magic wand, but it's not a Terminator.
lines.zh: AI不是魔法棒，也不是终结者。

lines.en: This system learns by accessing data.
lines.zh: 这个系统是通过访问数据来学习的。

lines.en: AI is not a harbinger of utopia or dystopia.
lines.zh: AI既不是乌托邦的预兆，也不是反乌托邦的预兆。

lines.en: And it changes its configuration in order to be able to predict stuff.
lines.zh: 它会调整自己的配置来预测东西。

lines.en: AI is something invented by us to advance our progress.
lines.zh: AI是我们发明出来推动进步的东西。

lines.en: It's really complicated, beautiful math,
lines.zh: 它是很复杂又漂亮的数学，

lines.en: but it is fundamentally just math.
lines.zh: 但归根到底就是数学。

lines.en: AI is deeply entangled with our desire to be in control,
lines.zh: AI和我们想掌控一切的欲望紧密相连。

lines.en: both of ourselves and the world around us.
lines.zh: 我们自己和周围的世界

lines.en: To control human behavior, to control the future,
lines.zh: 控制人的行为，掌握未来

lines.en: to control environmental outcomes,
lines.zh: 控制环境的结果

lines.en: to control institutions and societies.
lines.zh: 控制机构和社会

lines.en: AI is already here.
lines.zh: 人工智能已经来了

lines.zh: 它就在你的手机里

lines.en: Siri or Google or Alexa.
lines.zh: Siri、Google、Alexa

lines.en: Your cars.
lines.zh: 你的车

lines.en: Google Maps uses a very smart algorithm to provide...
lines.zh: 谷歌地图用很聪明的算法来提供...

lines.en: Your homes.
lines.zh: 你的家

lines.en: The home security system uses artificial intelligence
lines.zh: 家庭安防系统用上了人工智能

lines.en: to keep your family safe.
lines.zh: 来保护你家人的安全

lines.en: Your skies.
lines.zh: 你的天空

lines.en: From drones to unmanned aircrafts and beyond.
lines.zh: 从无人机到无人飞机等等

lines.en: Your hospitals.
lines.zh: 你的医院

lines.en: Interpreting x-rays.
lines.zh: 看懂X光片

lines.en: Your love lives.
lines.zh: 你的感情生活

lines.en: The dating apps.
lines.zh: 交友软件

lines.en: Your virtual lives.
lines.zh: 你的虚拟生活

lines.en: Social media companies spy on us.
lines.zh: 社交媒体公司在监视我们

lines.en: Your governments.
lines.zh: 你们的政府

lines.en: What it means for the future of our democracy.
lines.zh: 这对我们民主的未来意味着什么

lines.en: So we are, in fact, totally immersed in an AI world.
lines.zh: 所以我们其实已经完全生活在人工智能的世界里

clips.title: 美国人每天吃肉是怎么被“制造”出来的
clips.info_takeaway: 二战后，美国通过政府补贴、大规模养殖技术、冷藏普及和学校午餐计划，使得每日吃肉成为常态。

source.episode: The hidden forces shaping your choices

lines.en: In mid-century America, there was a strategy to make meat the norm, starting after the Second World War.
lines.zh: 在二十世纪中期的美国，有个策略是让吃肉变成常态，从二战结束后开始推行。

lines.en: As savory beef stew when it's armored.
lines.zh: 就像加了防护的咸香牛肉炖菜。

lines.en: It was impossible to avoid and it was advertised heavily and pushed on consumers.
lines.zh: 几乎避不开，到处打广告，拼命推给消费者。

lines.en: Hormel bacon stays fresh longer when saran wrapped.
lines.zh: 荷美尔的培根用保鲜膜包起来能放得更久。

lines.en: This is when they first started creating national ad campaigns.
lines.zh: 那时候他们才开始做全国性的广告宣传。

lines.en: And when you see these ads that meat is essential for your amino acids.
lines.zh: 你会看到这些广告说肉对你的氨基酸很重要。

lines.en: It's essential for a well-balanced diet.
lines.zh: 它是均衡饮食不可缺的。

lines.en: Found that man must have food which furnishes energy.
lines.zh: 发现人必须吃能提供能量的食物。

lines.en: Food to build muscle.
lines.zh: 能让肌肉增长的食物。

lines.en: And this is where we started getting this idea that meat was normal to eat every day in multiple meals.
lines.zh: 就是从这时候开始，我们觉得每天多顿吃肉是很正常的。

lines.en: When in reality, even ten years prior to that, it was an exception to have meat at a meal rather than the norm.
lines.zh: 其实在那之前十年，饭桌上有肉还是例外，不是常态。

lines.en: But three factors completely flipped what was for dinner.
lines.zh: 但有三个因素彻底改变了餐桌上的菜。

lines.en: First, the U.S.
lines.zh: 第一，美国

lines.en: government increased its meat subsidies.
lines.zh: 政府加大了对肉类的补贴。

lines.en: Second, new technology led to larger farms that could raise and slaughter more animals faster.
lines.zh: 第二，新技术让农场规模更大，养殖和屠宰速度更快。

lines.en: Plus, people could refrigerate and store more meat.
lines.zh: 而且人们可以冷藏、储存更多的肉。

lines.en: Finally, the National School Lunch Program launched.
lines.zh: 最后，全国学校午餐计划启动了。

lines.en: Lunches are designed to be nutritious.
lines.zh: 午餐是按营养来设计的。

lines.en: With a mandate to serve protein.
lines.zh: 规定必须有蛋白质。

lines.en: Meats and other foods rich in protein.
lines.zh: 比如肉类和其他高蛋白的食物。

lines.en: We saw the fastest and largest shift in our diets that has ever really happened.
lines.zh: 我们经历了史上最快、最大的一次饮食变化。

questions.question: What was one factor that contributed to making meat a daily norm in mid-century America?
options: A. The U.S. government increased its meat subsidies
B. People began importing more meat from overseas
C. Restaurants stopped serving vegetarian meals
D. Farmers switched entirely to poultry production
questions.explanation_zh: 文章提到政府增加肉类补贴是导致肉类成为日常饮食的重要因素之一。

questions.question: According to the passage, how were meats promoted to consumers after the Second World War?
options: A. Through national advertising campaigns emphasizing their nutritional value
B. By lowering prices through international trade agreements
C. By banning alternative protein sources
D. Through mandatory meat consumption laws
questions.explanation_zh: 文章明确指出在战后首次开展全国广告活动，强调肉类对氨基酸和均衡饮食的重要性。

clips.title: 一家人意外见证首颗原子弹试爆
clips.info_takeaway: 用户能了解到1945年美国首次原子弹试验（Trinity Test）的时间、地点，以及一个普通家庭偶然见证这一历史时刻的经历。

source.episode: Bright as Daybreak, Red as Fire

lines.en: My great-grandmother and great-grandfather,
lines.zh: 我的曾祖母和曾祖父

lines.en: they were ranchers in Southern New Mexico
lines.zh: 他们是在新墨西哥州南部的牧场主

lines.en: and they lived there their entire lives
lines.zh: 一辈子都住在那里

lines.en: out near the town of Las Cruces.
lines.zh: 在拉斯克鲁塞斯镇附近

lines.en: Tyler Johnson called our voicemail line
lines.zh: 泰勒·约翰逊打电话到我们的语音信箱

lines.en: to share a moment where his family members
lines.zh: 想分享他家人经历的一个瞬间

lines.en: had an accidental brush with history.
lines.zh: 他们无意间和历史擦肩而过

lines.en: They told us that in 1945,
lines.zh: 他们跟我们说在1945年

lines.en: they had gotten in the family car
lines.zh: 他们开着家里的车

lines.en: and driven to a town near the border of Arizona
lines.zh: 去了亚利桑那州边境附近的一个小镇

lines.en: and they had to stop for the night,
lines.zh: 他们不得不停下来过夜

lines.en: so they slept in their car on the side of the road.
lines.zh: 于是就在路边的车里睡了

lines.en: Early the next morning,
lines.zh: 第二天一大早

lines.en: they were awoken by a bright flash.
lines.zh: 他们被一道强光惊醒

lines.en: And that bright flash turned out to be the Trinity test,
lines.zh: 那道强光其实是“三位一体”核试验

lines.en: the first atomic bomb ever tested.
lines.zh: 这是第一次测试原子弹

lines.en: And they just happened to witness it in their car.
lines.zh: 他们就这么在车里碰巧看到了

lines.en: It was less than a month after the Trinity test
lines.zh: 不到一个月后

lines.en: that the U.S.
lines.zh: 美国

lines.en: dropped atomic bombs
lines.zh: 投下了原子弹

lines.en: on Hiroshima and Nagasaki.
lines.zh: 在广岛和长崎。

lines.en: That was the start of the atomic age
lines.zh: 那是原子时代的开始

lines.en: that continues to this day.
lines.zh: 一直延续到今天。

options: A. The Trinity test, the first atomic bomb ever tested

C. A sunrise
D. A fireworks display
questions.explanation_zh: 文中指出那道亮光是“三位一体”试验，即第一次原子弹测试。

clips.title: 冷战少年偷看机密地图的恐惧记忆
clips.info_takeaway: 用户能了解到冷战时期美国民众对核战争的恐惧，包括苏联攻击目标地图和核攻击时间预估等细节。

source.episode: Bright as Daybreak, Red as Fire

lines.en: When I really became conscious of the Cold War
lines.zh: 我真正意识到冷战的时候

lines.en: was in eighth grade.
lines.zh: 是在八年级。

lines.en: My dad was in the military and he had an office at home.
lines.zh: 我爸是军人，在家里有个办公室。

lines.en: That's John Conmey telling his kids what it was like
lines.zh: 这是约翰·康米在跟孩子们讲当时的情况

lines.en: growing up near Washington, D.C.
lines.zh: 他是在华盛顿特区附近长大的

lines.en: in the 60s.
lines.zh: 六十年代的事。

lines.en: We weren't supposed to be going in the office,
lines.zh: 我们本来不该进那个办公室

lines.en: but I was very curious.
lines.zh: 但我特别好奇。

lines.en: And on his desk was a classified document,
lines.zh: 他桌上放着一份机密文件

lines.en: a series of maps where the Soviets,
lines.zh: 是一系列苏联的地图

lines.en: if they bombed us, the targets they were going to hit.
lines.zh: 如果他们轰炸我们，那些他们要打的目标。

lines.en: I knew that if an attack came
lines.zh: 我知道如果真的遭到攻击

lines.en: and it would hit Washington, D.C.,
lines.zh: 而且会打到华盛顿特区

lines.en: there'd be about 15 minutes
lines.zh: 会有大约十五分钟

lines.en: before the first fallout would come.
lines.zh: 才会有第一波辐射尘到来

lines.en: And I had actually timed how long it would take
lines.zh: 我还真算过要花多久

lines.en: to run from school all the way home.
lines.zh: 才能从学校一路跑回家

lines.en: Not until I was grown did I feel comfortable
lines.zh: 直到长大我才觉得可以放心

lines.en: talking about the fear I experienced
lines.zh: 去谈我当时的恐惧

lines.en: growing up with the Cold War.
lines.zh: 在冷战中长大的那种害怕

lines.en: People don't understand how close we came
lines.zh: 人们不知道我们差点就到了那一步

lines.en: and have war broken out.
lines.zh: 战争差点爆发

lines.en: It would have been catastrophic.
lines.zh: 那会是场灾难

lines.en: We were at true risk of mankind as a species
lines.zh: 我们真的面临人类这个物种的风险

lines.en: being annihilated.
lines.zh: 差点被彻底消灭

questions.question: What did John Conmey find on his father's desk?
options: A. A classified document with maps showing Soviet bombing targets
B. A letter from the President about the Cold War
C. A diary describing military life
D. A newspaper article about Washington, D.C.

options: A. About 15 minutes

D. About 1 hour
questions.explanation_zh: 文中提到如果攻击发生，放射性尘埃到达华盛顿大约需要15分钟。

clips.title: 燃油飙升下的企业应对术
clips.info_takeaway: 燃油价格上涨导致运输成本增加，企业通过自提、合并订单等方式应对，但部分成本仍需转嫁给客户。

source.episode: Vietnam is in its workforce golden age

lines.en: the last few weeks Katherine Reynolds who handles imports for Palmetto Tile Distributors
lines.zh: 这几周，负责帕尔梅托瓷砖进口的凯瑟琳·雷诺兹

lines.en: in South Carolina, has been getting a lot of emails about fuel surcharges from shipping
lines.zh: 在南卡罗来纳州，收到不少关于运费附加的邮件

lines.en: companies.
lines.zh: 来自运输公司。

lines.en: So whatever, like, the gross bill is, they'll add 15 to 25 percent to that.
lines.zh: 不管账单是多少，他们都会再加上15%到25%。

lines.en: Reynolds says she's been through this before, when energy prices spiked after Russia invaded
lines.zh: 雷诺兹说她以前也遇到过这种情况，当时俄罗斯入侵

lines.en: Ukraine.
lines.zh: 乌克兰导致能源价格飙升。

lines.en: But Zach Rogers, a professor at Colorado State University, says back then, companies were
lines.zh: 但科罗拉多州立大学的扎克·罗杰斯教授说，那时候公司

lines.en: holding on to excess inventory they built up during the pandemic, and that helped them
lines.zh: 还留着疫情期间囤的库存，这帮他们

lines.en: to rely less on transportation.
lines.zh: 减少对运输的依赖。

lines.en: This year, we've been running inventories very lean.
lines.zh: 今年我们的库存一直很紧。

lines.en: And so even if transportation is very expensive, there will really still have to be some replenishment.
lines.zh: 所以就算运输很贵，还是得补货。

lines.en: Companies could build up their inventories again to avoid having to constantly replenish
lines.zh: 公司可以重新囤货，这样就不用一直补货

lines.en: items.
lines.zh: 商品。

lines.en: But Rogers says doing so comes with its own costs, since companies still have to pay tariffs
lines.zh: 但罗杰斯说，这也有成本，因为公司还是得付关税

lines.en: on imported goods.
lines.zh: 进口商品的关税。

lines.en: And so a lot of companies are finding themselves between a rock and a hard place because of
lines.zh: 所以很多公司因为这种关税和油价上涨的双重压力，进退两难

lines.en: these counteracting pressures of tariffs and surging fuel costs.
lines.zh: 关税和飙升的油价让他们左右为难。

lines.en: Many companies are doing what they can to mitigate those fuel costs.
lines.zh: 很多公司尽力想办法降低油费。

lines.en: Ken Gidden is CEO of Rothmans, a men's clothing store in New York.
lines.zh: 肯·吉登是纽约一家男装店Rothmans的CEO。

lines.en: He says he's also been getting hit with surcharges, even for local shipments.
lines.zh: 他说他连本地运输都要被收附加费。

lines.en: So you know, we started investigating alternatives.
lines.zh: 所以你知道，我们开始找别的办法了。

lines.en: And last week, we rented a truck and drove it out to the warehouse in New Jersey and
lines.zh: 上周我们租了辆卡车，开到新泽西的仓库，

lines.en: picked up 22 boxes.
lines.zh: 拉了 22 个箱子回来。

lines.en: Meanwhile, Catherine Reynolds at Palmetto Tile Distributors says she's trying to consolidate
lines.zh: 同时，Palmetto 瓷砖经销商的凯瑟琳·雷诺兹说，她正想把货合并一下，

lines.en: her shipments.
lines.zh: 一起发。

lines.zh: 我不知道。

lines.en: We just cry.
lines.zh: 我们就是会哭。

lines.en: We think about the Terminator.
lines.zh: 我们会想到《终结者》。

lines.en: We think about Ex Machina.
lines.zh: 我们会想到《机械姬》。

lines.en: You programmed her to flirt with me.
lines.zh: 你是故意让她跟我调情的。

lines.en: If I did, would that be cheating?
lines.zh: 如果我这么做，那算作弊吗？

lines.en: Wouldn't it?
lines.zh: 不是吗？

lines.en: We think about Star Trek.
lines.zh: 我们会想到《星际迷航》。

lines.en: Please state the nature of the medical emergency.
lines.zh: 请说明是什么医疗急症。

lines.en: Or maybe Star Wars.
lines.zh: 或者可能是《星球大战》。

lines.en: Hollywood is so deeply embedded in our brains.
lines.zh: 好莱坞已经深深刻在我们脑子里了。

lines.en: I'll be back.
lines.zh: 我会回来的。

lines.en: What does AI actually mean, you know?
lines.zh: 你知道，AI到底是什么意思？

lines.en: Really mean.
lines.zh: 真正的意思。

lines.en: AI is not a magic wand, but it's not a Terminator.
lines.zh: AI不是魔法棒，也不是终结者。

lines.en: The system learns by accessing data.
lines.zh: 这个系统是通过获取数据来学习的。

lines.en: AI is not a harbinger of utopia or dystopia.
lines.zh: AI不是乌托邦或反乌托邦的预兆。

lines.en: And it changes its configuration in order to be able to predict stuff.
lines.zh: 它会调整自己的结构来预测东西。

lines.en: AI is something invented by us to advance our progress.
lines.zh: AI是我们发明来推动进步的东西。

lines.en: It's really complicated, beautiful math, but it is fundamentally just math.
lines.zh: 它是很复杂又漂亮的数学，但本质上就是数学。

lines.en: AI is deeply entangled with our desire to be in control,
lines.zh: AI和我们想掌控的欲望紧密相连，

lines.en: both of ourselves and the world around us.
lines.zh: 既想掌控自己，也想掌控周围的世界。

lines.en: To control human behavior, to control the future,
lines.zh: 掌控人的行为，掌控未来，

lines.en: to control environmental outcomes, to control institutions and societies.
lines.zh: 掌控环境结果，掌控机构和社会。

lines.en: AI is already here.
lines.zh: AI已经存在了。

questions.question: What's the main point the speaker is making about AI?
options: A. AI is a human-made tool based on math, not a sci-fi fantasy
B. AI is dangerous and will soon take over the world
C. AI is mostly used for entertainment purposes
D. AI is still far from becoming a reality
questions.explanation_zh: 讲话者强调AI是人类创造的数学工具，而不是电影里的幻想或魔法。

questions.question: How does the speaker seem to feel about AI?
options: A. It's powerful but grounded in reality, and tied to our need for control
B. It's unpredictable and should be avoided at all costs
C. It's harmless and has no real impact on society
D. It's purely a threat to human freedom
questions.explanation_zh: 讲话者认为AI既有力量又现实，且与人类想控制世界的欲望密切相关。

clips.title: 被拒入境的美国公民
clips.info_takeaway: 一位在美国出生的华裔厨师回国探亲后被拒绝入境，引发公民身份争议。

source.episode: Who gets to be an American citizen?

lines.en: It all began when Wong Kim Ark a San Francisco born cook tried to return to California after visiting China his homeland
lines.zh: 故事开始于黄锦安，一个在旧金山出生的厨师，从中国老家回来想回到加州。

lines.en: I think when he saw San Francisco Bay emerge out of what was likely the foggy morning.
lines.zh: 我想他看到旧金山湾从大概是雾蒙蒙的早晨中显现出来时。

lines.en: This is Amanda Frost, professor of immigration law and author of
lines.zh: 这是阿曼达·弗罗斯特，她是移民法教授，也是一本书的作者。

lines.en: You Are Not American, Citizenship Stripping from Drutzka to the Dreamers.
lines.zh: 《你不是美国人：从德鲁兹卡到追梦人的公民身份剥夺》。

lines.en: He must have been thrilled to think, I'm finally back home and I can get off this boat and go back to my home in San Francisco.
lines.zh: 他一定很兴奋地想着：我终于回家了，可以下船回到旧金山的家。

lines.en: But that's not what would happen.
lines.zh: 但事情并没有按他想的那样发展。

lines.en: To Wong Kim Ark's surprise, a U.S.
lines.zh: 让黄锦安意外的是，美国

lines.en: customs agent declared that he was not allowed to get off the ship and set foot onto U.S.
lines.zh: 海关官员说他不能下船踏上美国

lines.en: soil.
lines.zh: 土地。

lines.en: At this point, the Chinese Exclusion Act was in effect.
lines.zh: 当时《排华法案》正在实施。

lines.en: And so if you were a Chinese laborer, you were not allowed to enter.
lines.zh: 所以如果你是中国劳工，就不准入境。

lines.en: Wong Kim Ark argued with the customs official.
lines.zh: 黄锦亚跟海关官员争论。

lines.en: He said, yes, I'm a laborer, I'm a chef, but I'm a citizen.
lines.zh: 他说，是的，我是劳工，我是厨师，但我是公民。

lines.en: And here's the proof.
lines.zh: 这是证据。

lines.en: He had his certificates.
lines.zh: 他有自己的证书。

lines.en: He knew that he was born in the United States and that meant he was a U.S.
lines.zh: 他知道自己出生在美国，这就意味着他是美国

lines.en: citizen.
lines.zh: 公民。

questions.question: What's the main point of this story about Wong Kim Ark?
options: A. A U.S.-born man was denied entry because of anti-Chinese laws despite being a citizen
B. A cook returned to San Francisco to open a restaurant
C. A customs agent helped a traveler find his documents
D. A professor explained how to apply for citizenship
questions.explanation_zh: 故事核心是讲一位在美国出生的华裔男子因排华法案被拒绝入境，尽管他是公民。

questions.question: How does the speaker seem to feel about Wong Kim Ark's situation?
options: A. It was unfair and highlights unjust treatment of citizens
B. It was a routine immigration check with no issues
C. It was a funny misunderstanding at the port
D. It was a necessary step to protect national security
questions.explanation_zh: 讲述者显然认为这种情况不公平，强调了对公民的不公正待遇。

clips.title: 科技巨头联手制定AI伦理
clips.info_takeaway: 2016年多家科技巨头成立人工智能合作组织，旨在确保AI用于增进而非剥削人类。

source.episode: Could AI help us, not replace us?

lines.en: At the time, were you worried
lines.zh: 当时你担心过吗

lines.en: about the ethical considerations
lines.zh: 关于伦理方面的问题

lines.en: since people did attribute human thinking to Siri?
lines.zh: 因为大家确实把 Siri 当成人在思考？

lines.en: I mean, did you start to think,
lines.zh: 我是说，你有没有开始觉得

lines.en: hmm, this is at the forefront
lines.zh: 嗯，这已经走在最前沿了

lines.en: of getting this kind of technology
lines.zh: 把这种技术

lines.en: into regular people's hands.
lines.zh: 带到普通人手里

lines.en: We really need to think about what's okay
lines.zh: 我们得认真想想什么可以做

lines.en: and what's not okay.
lines.zh: 什么不可以做

lines.en: Oh yeah, absolutely.
lines.zh: 哦，对啊，没错

lines.en: If people took Siri too seriously,
lines.zh: 如果有人太当真 Siri

lines.en: it means they're out there on the part of the curve
lines.zh: 那就说明他们处在曲线的某个位置

lines.en: where people are a bit inclined to see agency
lines.zh: 在那里的人更容易把东西看成有意图

lines.en: in inanimate objects more than other people.
lines.zh: 比别人更容易把无生命的东西当成有生命

lines.en: But in general, I saw that, yeah,
lines.zh: 但总体来说，我觉得是的

lines.en: of course, AI was moving on the head.
lines.zh: 当然，AI 正在快速发展

lines.en: While I was at Apple,
lines.zh: 我在苹果的时候

lines.en: the deep learning networks came to power.
lines.zh: 深度学习网络开始崛起

lines.en: And we were then saying,
lines.zh: 然后我们就说

lines.en: look, we've got to lay down the ethical foundation
lines.zh: 得先把伦理基础打好

lines.en: because this stuff is coming fast
lines.zh: 因为这东西来得很快

lines.en: and we need to make sure
lines.zh: 我们得确保

lines.en: that we don't use AI to exploit people,
lines.zh: 别用人工智能去剥削人

lines.en: that we use it to augment and work with people.
lines.zh: 而是用它来帮人、跟人合作

lines.en: In the fall of 2016, big tech companies,
lines.zh: 2016年秋天，大型科技公司

lines.en: including Amazon, Facebook, Google, IBM,
lines.zh: 包括亚马逊、Facebook、谷歌、IBM

lines.en: and Microsoft, competitors with each other,
lines.zh: 还有微软，虽然互相是竞争对手

lines.en: they came together to form a nonprofit
lines.zh: 他们联合成立了一个非营利组织

lines.en: called the Partnership on Artificial Intelligence
lines.zh: 叫做人工智能合作伙伴关系

lines.en: to Benefit People and Society.
lines.zh: 旨在造福人类和社会

questions.question: What's the main idea of this passage?
options: A. The speaker thinks AI's rapid growth requires clear ethical guidelines to protect people.
B. The speaker explains how Siri was programmed to mimic human thinking.
C. The speaker describes competition between tech companies in AI development.
D. The speaker talks about people's tendency to trust technology too much.
questions.explanation_zh: 整段主要强调人工智能发展很快，需要制定伦理规则来保护人类。

questions.question: How does the speaker feel about AI's future?
options: A. Excited but cautious, wanting AI to help rather than exploit people.
B. Completely unconcerned because AI is harmless.
C. Worried that AI will replace all human jobs soon.
D. Skeptical that AI will have any real impact.
questions.explanation_zh: 讲话者既看到AI的潜力，又强调必须谨慎使用，让AI帮助而不是利用人。

clips.title: 情绪如名琴需精心演奏
clips.info_takeaway: 斯特拉迪瓦里小提琴虽珍贵，但演奏不当会刺耳，情绪管理亦如此，需要练习才能优雅表达。

source.episode: A neuroscientist's guide to managing our emotions

lines.en: Not long ago, Sotheby's in New York held an auction for a Stradivarius.
lines.zh: 不久前，纽约的苏富比拍卖了一把斯特拉迪瓦里小提琴。

lines.en: The Joachim Ma Stradivarius, a masterpiece of sound.
lines.zh: 乔阿金·马的斯特拉迪瓦里，小提琴界的极品。

lines.en: And we can start the bidding here at $8 million, at $8 million, at $8 million.
lines.zh: 我们从八百万美元起拍，八百万，八百万。

lines.en: The bids rose quickly.
lines.zh: 竞价很快就涨了。
八百五十万美元。

lines.en: Some from anonymous clients phoning in.
lines.zh: 有些是匿名客户打电话来的。

lines.en: $9 million.
lines.zh: 九百万美元。

lines.en: I have Ella at $9 million, at $9 million already.
lines.zh: 艾拉已经出到九百万了，九百万。

lines.en: The numbers went up and up until...
lines.zh: 数字一路往上飙，直到……

lines.en: $10 million.
lines.zh: 一千万美元。

lines.en: So what makes a Stradivarius so valuable?
lines.zh: 那斯特拉迪瓦里小提琴为什么这么值钱？

lines.en: Well, for one thing, they're very rare.
lines.zh: 首先，它们很稀有。

lines.en: Only about 600 Stradivarius violins are believed to exist today.
lines.zh: 现在世界上大概只剩六百把斯特拉迪瓦里小提琴。

lines.en: But perhaps more important are the sounds that they produce.
lines.zh: 更重要的是它们发出的声音。

lines.en: Musicians swear by their exquisite craftsmanship and
lines.zh: 音乐家都夸它的工艺精湛，

lines.en: say no other violin can match their rich tones.
lines.zh: 说没有其他小提琴能比得上它的音色。

lines.en: A Stradivarius is this instrument that is capable of creating this
lines.zh: 斯特拉迪瓦里小提琴能演奏出那种

lines.en: magical music that transports us and creates beauty in the world for
lines.zh: 像魔法一样的音乐，让人沉醉、让世界更美。

lines.en: those who listen to it and for the person playing it as well.
lines.zh: 听的人和演奏的人都会被它打动。

lines.en: But here's the thing.
lines.zh: 不过重点是——

lines.en: Even a Stradivarius can sound terrible if it's played poorly.
lines.zh: 就算是斯特拉迪瓦里小提琴，拉得不好也会很难听。

lines.en: Have you ever heard someone play a violin the wrong way?
lines.zh: 你有没有听过有人小提琴拉错方法的？

lines.en: Oh, yeah, it's painful.
lines.zh: 哦，是啊，真让人难受。

lines.en: Please stop.
lines.zh: 别拉了。

lines.en: Painful is the perfect word, right?
lines.zh: “难受”这个词用得太贴切了，对吧？

lines.en: Because if you don't know how to play that instrument, it can cause pain.
lines.zh: 因为你不会演奏的话，那乐器会让人很痛苦。

lines.en: And that's true of our emotions as well.
lines.zh: 我们的情绪也是一样的。

questions.question: What's the main point of this passage?
options: A. A Stradivarius is rare and beautiful, but its magic depends on how it's played.
B. Stradivarius violins are expensive because they are old.
C. Playing violin well requires years of practice.
D. Auctions are exciting events with high bids.
questions.explanation_zh: 文章主要强调斯特拉迪瓦里小提琴虽稀有且音色优美，但若演奏不好就无法展现其魅力。

questions.question: What does the speaker suggest about the lesson from this story?
options: A. Just like violins, emotions can cause beauty or pain depending on how we handle them.
B. Expensive things always sound better.
C. Musical talent is more important than emotions.
D. Rare items should be kept in museums.
questions.explanation_zh: 讲者用小提琴作比喻，说明情绪如同乐器，处理得当能带来美好，处理不当则会带来痛苦。

clips.title: 车中过夜遇见原子弹试验
clips.info_takeaway: 1945年一家人在车中过夜，意外目睹了首次原子弹试验的闪光。

source.episode: Bright as Daybreak, Red as Fire

lines.en: My great-grandmother and great-grandfather,
lines.zh: 我曾祖母和曾祖父

lines.en: they were ranchers in Southern New Mexico
lines.zh: 他们是在新墨西哥州南部养牛的

lines.en: and they lived there their entire lives
lines.zh: 一辈子都住在那里

lines.en: out near the town of Las Cruces.
lines.zh: 在拉斯克鲁塞斯镇附近

lines.en: Tyler Johnson called our voicemail line
lines.zh: 泰勒·约翰逊打到我们的语音信箱

lines.en: to share a moment where his family members
lines.zh: 想分享一次他家人的经历

lines.en: had an accidental brush with history.
lines.zh: 那是一次意外碰上历史的时刻

lines.en: They told us that in 1945,
lines.zh: 他们跟我们说，1945 年的时候

lines.en: they had gotten in the family car
lines.zh: 他们开着家里的车

lines.en: and driven to a town near the border of Arizona
lines.zh: 去了亚利桑那州边境附近的一个小镇

lines.en: and they had to stop for the night,
lines.zh: 他们不得不停下来过夜

lines.en: so they slept in their car on the side of the road.
lines.zh: 于是就在路边的车里睡了

lines.en: Early the next morning,
lines.zh: 第二天一大早

lines.en: they were awoken by a bright flash.
lines.zh: 他们被一道强光惊醒

lines.en: And that bright flash turned out to be the Trinity test,
lines.zh: 那道强光其实是“三位一体”核试验

lines.en: the first atomic bomb ever tested.
lines.zh: 这是第一次测试原子弹

lines.en: And they just happened to witness it in their car.
lines.zh: 他们就这么在车里碰巧看到了

lines.en: It was less than a month after the Trinity test
lines.zh: 不到一个月后

lines.en: that the U.S.
lines.zh: 美国

lines.en: dropped atomic bombs
lines.zh: 投下了原子弹

lines.en: on Hiroshima and Nagasaki.
lines.zh: 在广岛和长崎

lines.en: That was the start of the atomic age
lines.zh: 那是原子时代的开始

lines.en: that continues to this day.
lines.zh: 一直延续到今天

questions.question: What's the main point of this story?
options: A. A family accidentally witnessed a historic atomic bomb test.
B. A family moved to Las Cruces to start a ranch.
C. The U.S. military invited locals to watch the Trinity test.
D. A family took a road trip to Arizona to visit relatives.
questions.explanation_zh: 故事主要讲的是一家人在旅行中无意间目睹了历史性的原子弹试验。

questions.question: How does the speaker seem to feel about the event?
options: A. It was a surprising and memorable brush with history.
B. It was a dangerous and frightening experience they regret.
C. It was an ordinary roadside stop with nothing special.
D. It was a planned visit to witness a scientific milestone.
questions.explanation_zh: 说话者强调这是一次意外但令人难忘的历史接触，带有惊讶和回忆的情感。

clips.title: 濒死后的选择
clips.info_takeaway: 叙述者回忆在枪击中濒死的经历，以及康复期间决定不被恐惧和愤怒支配。

source.episode: What it Takes to Heal

lines.zh: 丹能问出这个问题真不容易，因为他是个幸存者

lines.zh: 2018年匹兹堡犹太教堂枪击案，那是美国史上对犹太人最严重的袭击

lines.en: history.

lines.zh: 他腹部中枪，被丢下等死

lines.en: even people he might disagree with.

questions.question: What's the main point of this story about Dan?
options: A. It's about surviving a violent attack and choosing a path toward understanding rather than fear.
B. It's about how hospitals treat victims of gun violence.
C. It's about the history of attacks on Jewish people in America.

questions.question: How does Dan feel about life after the attack?

D. He feels hopeless and sees no way to heal.

clips.title: 泰勒·斯威夫特如何推动经济
clips.info_takeaway: 主持人通过社交媒体帖子讲述美联储加息与经济韧性，点出文化事件对经济的推动作用。

source.episode: "Marketplace" Live

lines.en: A couple of years ago, I was at work scrolling through my Instagram.
lines.zh: 几年前，我上班的时候在刷 Instagram。

lines.en: Yes, I was at work scrolling Instagram, don't tell my bosses.
lines.zh: 是的，我上班在刷 Instagram，别告诉我老板。

lines.en: But the excuse is it was Fed Day.
lines.zh: 不过我有借口，那天是美联储日。

lines.en: The Federal Reserve had that day, raised its benchmark interest rate,
lines.zh: 那天美联储把基准利率调高了，

lines.en: the federal funds rate, by 25 basis points, a quarter of one percentage point.
lines.zh: 联邦基金利率上调了25个基点，也就是0.25%。

lines.en: And that was a big deal because if you remember back then, 2023-ish,
lines.zh: 这事挺大，因为如果你记得当时，大概是2023年左右，

lines.en: the middle of that year, inflation was going up, people a little agitated.
lines.zh: 那年中期，通胀在上涨，大家有点烦躁。

lines.en: COVID was still not completely in the rearview mirror.
lines.zh: 新冠还没完全过去。

lines.en: And it was a very big deal because the Fed also said that day,
lines.zh: 而且更重要的是，美联储那天还说，

lines.en: we don't think there's going to be a recession.
lines.zh: 我们觉得不会有经济衰退。

lines.en: And that was good news.
lines.zh: 这可是个好消息。

lines.en: So there I am, scrolling my Instagram, and this post pops up.
lines.zh: 我正刷着 Instagram，突然看到一条帖子。

lines.en: The Federal Reserve is no longer forecasting a recession.
lines.zh: 美联储不再预测会有经济衰退。

lines.en: And we just wanted to thank some of the economic stars that got us to where we are today.
lines.zh: 我们就是想感谢一些让我们走到今天的经济明星。

lines.en: First off, Taylor Swift.
lines.zh: 首先，泰勒·斯威夫特。

lines.en: You bought tickets, you booked hotels,
lines.zh: 你买了票，还订了酒店。

lines.en: and you rode those lift scooters in the middle of the street quite dangerously.
lines.zh: 还在大街上骑着滑板车，挺危险的。

lines.en: Most importantly, you boosted TDP.
lines.zh: 最重要的是，你拉高了 GDP。

questions.explanation_zh: 讲话者既觉得这是好消息，也对将经济改善归功于流行歌手的帖子感到有趣。

clips.title: 员工眼中的私募收购
clips.info_takeaway: Cindy在安全设备公司工作多年，担心被KKR收购会导致裁员或外迁，这是私募收购常见担忧。

source.episode: A pro-worker experiment in private equity

lines.en: Cindy Cordes loved her job.
lines.zh: 辛迪·科尔德斯很喜欢她的工作。

lines.en: She worked at a company called Capital Safety.
lines.zh: 她在一家叫资本安全的公司上班。

lines.en: It made safety equipment like harnesses people wear,
lines.zh: 公司做安全设备，比如人穿的安全带，

lines.en: washing windows on skyscrapers, or working on oil rigs.
lines.zh: 用来擦高楼的窗户，或者在油井平台上干活。

lines.en: And her team made the part that attaches to the harnesses.
lines.zh: 她的团队负责做连接安全带的那部分。

lines.en: I was in the shocks, which the shocks was the part that would tie off from the harness
lines.zh: 我负责做减震器，就是把安全带固定起来的那部分。

lines.en: to your point of, say, on a building or a scaffolding or whatever.
lines.zh: 固定到建筑、脚手架之类的地方。

lines.en: What was your favorite part of the job?
lines.zh: 你最喜欢这份工作的哪部分？

lines.en: Sending out quality equipment and knowing that it's going to save people lives.
lines.zh: 发出去的设备质量好，而且知道它能救人命。

lines.en: She'd been at the company since the early 90s, worked her way up.
lines.zh: 她从九十年代初就在这家公司，一路干到现在。

lines.en: And by 2011, Cindy was the manufacturing lead on the production floor.
lines.zh: 到2011年，Cindy已经是生产车间的制造主管了。

lines.en: She oversaw a team of like 40 people.
lines.zh: 她带着大概40人的团队。

lines.en: And I had to make sure that they had their job orders for the day,
lines.zh: 我得确保他们当天的工作单都准备好了，

lines.en: that they had all their materials so that they could make their equipment for the day.
lines.zh: 材料也齐全，这样他们才能做当天的设备。

lines.en: And if there was any issues, they would come to me and I would try to solve them.
lines.zh: 要是有问题，他们会来找我，我会尽量解决。

lines.en: And one day that year, she hears that her company is getting sold.
lines.zh: 那年有一天，她听说公司要被卖了。

lines.en: It was like, OK, now what are we going to be getting into?
lines.zh: 就像，嗯，那我们接下来要面对什么？

lines.en: The company that's buying her company, KKR, a private equity firm, one of the big ones,
lines.zh: 买她公司的是KKR，一家大型私募股权公司。

lines.en: which is not always good news.
lines.zh: 这可不总是好消息。

lines.en: When you heard that you were getting bought by private equity, were you like, oh, great?
lines.zh: 你听说要被私募收购时，是不是觉得，哦，太好了？

lines.en: Um, mixed feelings.
lines.zh: 嗯，心里挺复杂的。

lines.en: You know, one big fear is you're like, oh, are they going to take it, you know,
lines.zh: 你知道，有个大担心就是，哎，他们会不会把它拿走，

lines.en: and take it overseas, close the company here?
lines.zh: 然后搬到国外，把这边公司关了？

lines.en: You know, you had all those worries, you know, when you have a bigger company buy you.
lines.zh: 你知道，被大公司收购的时候这些担心都会有。

questions.question: What's the main point of Cindy's story?
options: A. She loved her job making safety equipment but was worried when a big firm bought her company.
B. She decided to quit her job after many years in manufacturing.
C. She was excited about moving the company overseas.
D. She wanted to start her own safety equipment business.

questions.question: How did Cindy feel about the private equity takeover?

questions.explanation_zh: 她既有复杂情绪，也担心收购可能带来不利变化，比如搬到海外或关闭。

lines.en: In Apple's case, that assistant will be a significantly upgraded Siri coming in iOS 27.
lines.zh: 苹果的语音助手会是升级版 Siri，随 iOS 27 推出

lines.en: The glasses are part of a broader three-pronged AI wearable strategy
lines.zh: 这款眼镜是苹果三大 AI 可穿戴战略的一部分

lines.en: that also includes new AirPods and a camera-equipped pendant.
lines.zh: 还包括新款 AirPods 和带摄像头的挂件。

lines.en: Each device is designed to leverage computer vision to interpret the user's surroundings
lines.zh: 每个设备都用计算机视觉来理解用户周围环境。

lines.en: and feed contextual awareness into Siri and Apple Intelligence.
lines.zh: 然后把这些环境信息传给 Siri 和 Apple Intelligence。

lines.en: That will enable features like improved turn-by-turn map directions and visual reminders.
lines.zh: 这样就能实现更精准的地图导航和视觉提醒等功能。

lines.en: According to employees working on the project,
lines.zh: 据参与这个项目的员工说，

lines.en: Apple's strategy is to outdo competitors by tightly integrating the glasses with the iPhone
lines.zh: 苹果的策略是把眼镜和 iPhone 深度结合来超越竞争对手。

lines.en: and offering a higher-end build.
lines.zh: 并且提供更高端的做工。

lines.en: While Meta relies heavily on partner Essilor Luxottica SA for frames,
lines.zh: Meta 很依赖合作伙伴 Essilor Luxottica SA 来做镜框，

lines.en: Apple is unsurprisingly planning to go it alone in terms of design.
lines.zh: 而苹果则打算自己设计，不出意料。

lines.en: That should also set it apart from Alphabet's Google and Samsung Electronics,
lines.zh: 这也会让它和谷歌、三星电子有所区别。

lines.en: which are leaning on Warby Parker.
lines.zh: 它们靠在 Warby Parker 上。

lines.en: Apple's design team has whipped up at least four different styles
lines.zh: 苹果的设计团队搞出了至少四种不同的款式

lines.en: and plans to launch some or all of them, I'm told, as well as many color options.
lines.zh: 据我说，他们打算推出其中一些或全部，还有很多颜色可选。

lines.en: The latest units are made from a high-end material.
lines.zh: 最新的设备用的是高档材料。

clips.title: Meta MuseSpark战略布局

source.episode: Meta’s Refreshed AI Play

lines.en: The release offers an early indication of how Mark Zuckerberg plans to incorporate Meta's
lines.zh: 这次发布提前透露了扎克伯格打算怎么把 Meta 的

lines.en: social media content into its chatbot as part of his promise to develop
lines.zh: 社交媒体内容整合进聊天机器人，这是他承诺要开发

lines.en: what he calls personal superintelligence.
lines.zh: 他所谓的“个人超级智能”的一部分。

lines.zh: MuseSpark 在部分测试中击败了谷歌、OpenAI 和 Anthropic 的顶尖模型

lines.en: on reasoning and multimodal capabilities, according to evaluations shared by Meta.
lines.zh: 在推理和多模态能力方面，这是 Meta 分享的评估结果。

lines.en: But it acknowledged that the model was not yet cutting edge in certain areas, adding,
lines.zh: 但他们也承认这个模型在某些领域还不是最先进，并补充说

lines.zh: 我们会继续投资在目前表现有差距的领域

lines.en: specifically long-horizon agentic systems and coding workflows.
lines.zh: 尤其是长周期自主系统和编程流程。

lines.en: Zuckerberg said on Wednesday that Meta planned to release increasingly
lines.zh: 扎克伯格周三表示，Meta 计划推出越来越

lines.en: advanced models that push the frontier of intelligence and capabilities,
lines.zh: 先进的模型，推动智能和能力的边界。

lines.en: including new open-source models.

lines.en: We are building products that don't just answer your questions,

lines.en: but act as agents that do things for you, he added in a post on Threads.

lines.en: With MuseSpark, Meta appears to lean into niche capabilities such as health,

lines.en: arguing that it is one of the top reasons people turn to AI.

lines.en: Rival OpenAI in January launched ChatGPT Health, a specialized feature that allows users to connect
lines.zh: 竞争对手 OpenAI 在一月推出了 ChatGPT Health，这个功能让用户可以

lines.en: their medical records and fitness apps to its AI to get personalized health advice.
lines.zh: 把医疗记录和健身应用连接到它的 AI，获得个性化健康建议。

lines.en: Meta has also been working to improve the model's multimodal perception,
lines.zh: Meta 也在努力提升模型的多模态感知能力，

lines.en: such as recognizing objects in photos.

lines.en: Zuckerberg last year said Meta was doubling down on its investment in AI glasses,
lines.zh: 扎克伯格去年说，Meta 正加倍投资 AI 眼镜。

lines.en: arguing wearable devices are vital to his bet on superintelligence.

lines.en: When Meta AI, powered by MuseSpark, comes to our AI glasses,
lines.zh: 等 Meta AI 搭载 MuseSpark 上我们的 AI 眼镜时，

lines.en: the assistant will be able to better see and understand the world around you,
lines.zh: 助手就能更好地看懂你周围的世界，

clips.title: 女性药物试验缺席的真相
clips.info_takeaway: 直到1993年，美国才强制要求在政府资助的医学试验中纳入女性，源于早期药物安全事件。

source.episode: Is there still a gender gap in medical research?

lines.en: So Emily, did you know that it wasn't mandatory to include women in medical trials funded
lines.zh: 艾米莉，你知道在医学试验里，女性以前不是必须要参加的吗？

lines.en: by the National Institutes of Health until 1993?
lines.zh: 直到1993年，美国国立卫生研究院资助的试验才规定必须包括女性。

lines.en: Wait, medical trials like drug trials?
lines.zh: 等等，你是说像药物试验那种吗？

lines.en: Yeah, partially.
lines.zh: 对，部分是这样。

lines.en: And these trials are really important.
lines.zh: 这些试验可很重要。

lines.en: The NIH is the largest single public funder of biomedical research in the world.
lines.zh: NIH是全球最大的公共生物医学研究资助机构。

lines.en: Like I'm a doctor, right?
lines.zh: 就像我是医生，对吧？

lines.en: So I'm constantly looking at results of research on different drugs or treatments.
lines.zh: 所以我一直在看各种药物或治疗的研究结果。

lines.en: And this helps me decide if a test or medicine I'm using is safe or effective for my patients.
lines.zh: 这些结果能帮我判断我用的检测或药物对病人是不是安全有效。

lines.en: And you're saying it wasn't mandatory for women to be included in those until the 1990s?
lines.zh: 你是说直到90年代才强制把女性纳入这些试验？

lines.en: Yeah, we probably need some backstory here.
lines.zh: 嗯，我们可能得先讲点背景。

lines.en: So there was this global scandal starting in the late 1950s where tens of thousands
lines.zh: 事情是从1950年代末开始的，一个全球性的丑闻。

lines.en: of pregnant women, mainly in Europe, took this sedative called thalidomide for morning sickness.
lines.zh: 很多孕妇，主要在欧洲，为了缓解孕吐吃了一种叫反应停的镇静药。

lines.en: People who took the drug while pregnant ended up having babies whose limbs were poorly developed
lines.zh: 怀孕期间吃了这种药的人，生出来的孩子四肢发育不良

lines.en: or even absent.
lines.zh: 甚至没有四肢。

lines.en: This happened to over 12,000 kids.
lines.zh: 这种情况发生在一万两千多个孩子身上。

lines.en: And it led to the Food and Drug Administration creating a policy excluding, quote,
lines.zh: 这事促使美国食品药品管理局出台了一个政策，规定

lines.en: women of childbearing potential in early drug trials.
lines.zh: 早期药物试验里排除有生育能力的女性。

lines.en: That was in 1977.
lines.zh: 那是在1977年。

lines.en: Oh, so they just excluded most women.
lines.zh: 哦，所以他们基本把大多数女性都排除了。

lines.en: Yeah, exactly.
lines.zh: 对，没错。

lines.en: But that's not the only reason that women have been historically excluded from biomedical research.
lines.zh: 但这并不是女性长期被排除在生物医学研究之外的唯一原因。

lines.en: Women have been seen as either too kind of variable, hormonally, like, noisy and therefore
lines.zh: 女性被认为激素变化太多，数据太乱，所以

lines.en: bad sources of data.
lines.zh: 不适合当数据来源。

lines.en: And or we have just taken a male body as a standard and used that to produce knowledge
lines.zh: 或者我们干脆把男性身体当作标准，用它来生成知识

lines.en: for everyone.
lines.zh: 然后套用到所有人身上。

lines.en: So that's Marina DeMarco.
lines.zh: 这位是玛丽娜·德马可。

lines.en: She's a philosopher of science at Washington University in St.
lines.zh: 她是圣路易斯华盛顿大学的科学哲学家。

lines.en: Louis.
lines.zh: 在圣路易斯。

lines.en: And she told us that over time, scientists and activists realized that there was this
lines.zh: 她跟我们说，随着时间推移，科学家和活动人士意识到

lines.en: huge gap in data for research on women.
lines.zh: 在女性研究的数据上有很大缺口。

lines.en: They started pushing for more inclusive policies.
lines.zh: 他们开始推动更包容的政策。

lines.en: And there were even protests about this in the 1980s and 90s.
lines.zh: 在八九十年代甚至还有人为了这事抗议。

lines.en: And Congress listened.
lines.zh: 国会听进去了。

lines.en: They passed a law in 1993 requiring researchers to include women and people of color in federally
lines.zh: 1993年他们通过了一条法律，要求研究人员在联邦

lines.en: funded medical trials.
lines.zh: 资助的医疗试验中必须包括女性和有色人种。

questions.question: What's the main point of this conversation?
options: A. Women were long excluded from medical trials, and laws eventually changed to require their inclusion.
B. The NIH stopped funding drug trials in the 1990s.
C. Thalidomide was the only drug ever tested on pregnant women.
D. Hormonal differences make women unsuitable for medical research.
questions.explanation_zh: 对话主要讲的是女性长期被排除在医学试验之外，最终法律改变要求必须纳入女性。

questions.question: How does the speaker feel about including women in medical research?
options: A. It's essential to fill the data gap and ensure treatments are safe for everyone.
B. It's risky and should be avoided.
C. It's only useful for pregnancy-related drugs.
D. It doesn't make much difference to research outcomes.
questions.explanation_zh: 讲话者认为纳入女性是必要的，可以弥补数据缺口并确保治疗对所有人安全有效。

clips.title: 40%人排便影响生活
clips.info_takeaway: 40%美国人排便习惯影响生活，医生建议了解身体运作可自行解决部分问题。

source.episode: Are you pooping all wrong?

lines.en: you regular
lines.zh: 你排便规律吗

lines.en: That's a question that I remember hearing from older movies or sitcoms when I was a
lines.zh: 这问题我记得小时候在老电影或情景剧里听过

lines.en: kid and it took me a while to understand what it meant.
lines.zh: 那时候我花了好一阵才明白意思

lines.en: That it was a delicate question to ask if your bathroom schedule was regular or disrupting
lines.zh: 其实是委婉地问你上厕所是不是规律

lines.en: your life.
lines.zh: 有没有影响到生活

lines.en: 40% of Americans report that their bowel habits disrupt their daily lives.
lines.zh: 有40%的美国人说排便习惯影响了日常生活

lines.en: Wow.
lines.zh: 哇

lines.en: Isn't that a huge number?
lines.zh: 这数字是不是很大

lines.en: That's like almost half of us.
lines.zh: 差不多快一半人了

lines.en: That's gastroenterologist and medical journalist Dr.
lines.zh: 这是胃肠科医生兼医学记者

lines.en: Trisha Pasricha, who's taken it upon
lines.zh: Trisha Pasricha 自告奋勇

lines.en: herself to help this 40% of people who need help.
lines.zh: 去帮那 40% 需要帮助的人。

lines.en: There's a lot that we just don't talk about, don't know, and if we could just
lines.zh: 有很多事我们不聊、不懂，如果我们能

lines.en: all of us lean in a little bit more to our physiology and know how our body is supposed
lines.zh: 多关注一下自己的身体，了解它该怎么

lines.en: to work, then I think we could solve a lot of this problem on our own.
lines.zh: 运作，我觉得很多问题我们自己就能解决。

lines.en: Trisha's latest push to solve this problem is a new book called You've Been Pooping
lines.zh: Trisha 最近为解决这个问题推出了一本新书，叫《你一直在错误地排便》

lines.en: All Wrong, and she's never been shy when it comes to talking about, well, poop.
lines.zh: 她谈到便便从来不害羞。

lines.en: I grew up in a poop positive family and I hope I am currently raising a poop positive
lines.zh: 我在一个对便便很开放的家庭长大，现在也希望自己在养育一个对便便很开放的

lines.en: family, but my dad was and is also a neuro gastroenterologist, so we talked about poop
lines.zh: 家庭，而且我爸过去和现在都是神经胃肠科医生，所以我们经常聊便便

lines.en: all the time.
lines.zh: 聊得很频繁。

lines.en: I was the same.
lines.zh: 我也是这样。

lines.en: Growing up in my family, we talked about going number two all the time, but for a lot of
lines.zh: 我家从小就经常聊上厕所的事，但对很多人来说

lines.en: people, the education we get when it comes to going to the bathroom, it stops once we're
lines.zh: 上厕所的知识基本在幼儿期就停了

lines.en: out of our potty training phase, around two and a half or three years old.
lines.zh: 差不多两岁半到三岁学会用便盆就结束了。

lines.en: It's a huge issue.
lines.zh: 这是个大问题。

lines.en: I mean, for example, a lot of people can't poop at work.
lines.zh: 比如很多人上班根本拉不出来。

lines.en: Well, a lot of us who don't have the luxury of working from home, the urge to go is going
lines.zh: 我们这些不能在家办公的人，想上厕所的感觉迟早会来

lines.en: to strike.
lines.zh: 一定会有那一刻。

lines.en: And like, if you have decided that you just can't go, you're constipating yourself almost
lines.zh: 如果你决定就是不去，那其实是自己让自己便秘

lines.en: by choice and you're creating a big problem for yourself later.
lines.zh: 等于自找麻烦，后面会更糟。

lines.en: Today on the show, for your health and happiness, let's make pooping less taboo.
lines.zh: 今天节目里，为了你的健康和快乐，我们来让上厕所不再那么尴尬。

clips.title: 排便“三个P”之柔软度
clips.info_takeaway: 医生提出“三个P”框架，首个是排便的柔软度，纤维摄入不足是常见原因。

source.episode: Are you pooping all wrong?

lines.en: OK, so Dr.
lines.zh: 好，那医生。

lines.en: Trisha, can I call you that?
lines.zh: Trisha，我可以这么叫你吗？

lines.en: Please.
lines.zh: 当然。

lines.en: Dr.
lines.zh: 医生。

lines.en: Trisha, you write that the heart of why people are pooping wrong starts with the fact that as a society,
lines.zh: Trisha，你写过，人们拉屎方式不对的根源是因为我们这个社会

lines.en: we do not talk about pooping.
lines.zh: 不谈拉屎这事。

lines.en: I mean, other than me, I talk about pooping all the time.
lines.zh: 除了我，我几乎天天聊拉屎。

lines.en: We're trying to change that today, starting with like, how are people pooping wrong?
lines.zh: 我们今天想改变这一点，先从人们是怎么拉错开始。

lines.en: Well, I think that people don't have a good framework for how to poop.
lines.zh: 我觉得大家没有一个正确的拉屎方法。

lines.en: And so I think about it in terms of three things.
lines.zh: 所以我把它分成三个方面来想。

lines.en: And I call these the three Ps.
lines.zh: 我把这叫做三个P。

lines.en: But one, I think that pliability is off.
lines.zh: 不过我觉得柔软度不太够。

lines.en: And that is how basically how soft or pillowy that poop is, pliability.
lines.zh: 就是便便有多软、多像枕头一样，这就是柔软度。

lines.en: More fiber.
lines.zh: 多吃点纤维。

lines.en: Often, but not always, but often the issue is fiber.
lines.zh: 很多时候问题就是纤维不足，但也不总是。

lines.en: And truthfully, 95% of Americans, we are not meeting our fiber goal.
lines.zh: 说实话，美国人有95%都没达到纤维摄入目标。

lines.en: So like, more often than not, you need more fiber.
lines.zh: 所以多数情况下，你需要多吃纤维。

lines.en: Propulsion is the other problem.
lines.zh: 另一个问题是推进力。

lines.en: Propulsion refers to how your muscles are generating these contractions in your own gut to push that poop forward.
lines.zh: 推进力就是肠道肌肉收缩，把便便往前推的过程。

lines.en: So that's the second P.
lines.zh: 这就是第二个P。

questions.question: What's the main idea of this conversation?
options: A. People often poop incorrectly because society avoids talking about it and lacks a clear framework.
B. Fiber supplements are the best solution for all digestive problems.
C. Everyone already knows how to poop properly but ignores it.
D. Pooping issues are mostly caused by drinking too little water.
questions.explanation_zh: 对话主要强调人们排便方式不正确的核心原因是社会不讨论这个话题，缺乏明确的指导框架。

questions.question: How does the speaker seem to feel about discussing pooping?
options: A. They think it's important and want to break the taboo.
B. They feel embarrassed and prefer to avoid it.
C. They believe only doctors should talk about it.
D. They think it's a minor issue not worth attention.
questions.explanation_zh: 说话者希望改变社会对排便的沉默，认为公开讨论很重要。

clips.title: 石油巨头为何要多造塑料
clips.info_takeaway: 环保记者震惊发现石油巨头正投入巨资增加塑料产量，与减少塑料的公众努力背道而驰。

source.episode: Who's to blame for all this plastic?

lines.en: And then one day I read an article and it just was like a total gut punch because the
lines.zh: 有一天我看了一篇文章，整个人都被震到了，因为

lines.en: article said that huge fossil fuel and petrochemical companies like Exxon Mobil and their peers
lines.zh: 文章说像埃克森美孚这种大型化石燃料和石化公司

lines.en: in the industry were ramping up to actually increase plastic production.
lines.zh: 正准备大幅增加塑料产量。

lines.en: Plastics come from petrochemicals, which are made by the fossil fuel industry.
lines.zh: 塑料是由石化产品制成的，而石化产品来自化石燃料行业。

lines.en: And reading the news that the industry planned to make even more plastic stopped Beth in
lines.zh: 看到行业还打算生产更多塑料的新闻，让贝丝一下愣住了。

lines.en: her tracks.
lines.zh: 她停下了手里的事。

lines.en: To then learn that, you know, this gigantic, wealthy, super politically powerful industry
lines.zh: 后来她得知，这个庞大、富有、政治影响力极强的行业

lines.en: was actually pushing really hard in the other direction and pouring billions of dollars
lines.zh: 其实在拼命往相反方向走，还投入了数十亿美元

lines.en: into new manufacturing facilities and that their plans were to make even more plastic
lines.zh: 建新的生产设施，而且计划未来生产更多塑料。

lines.en: in the future.
lines.zh: 打算以后还要继续增加塑料产量。

lines.en: It just, you know, was such a shock.
lines.zh: 这事儿真是让人震惊，你懂的。

lines.en: And this revelation, Beth says, planted the seed for her new book, Plastic, Inc.
lines.zh: 贝丝说，这个发现让她萌生了写新书《塑料公司》的想法。

lines.en: Plastic as a revenue stream is helping to float the fossil fuel industry and keep it
lines.zh: 塑料作为收入来源，正在帮化石燃料行业撑着。

lines.en: going as it starts to be undersold by clean energy.
lines.zh: 因为清洁能源开始抢市场，它还能继续运转。

lines.en: So any additional source of money is a way to keep drilling.
lines.zh: 所以多一份收入，就能继续钻油。

questions.question: What's the main point of the speaker's story?
options: A. Big fossil fuel companies are increasing plastic production to stay profitable.
B. Plastic production is declining due to clean energy competition.
C. Beth decided to quit using plastic after reading an article.
D. Petrochemical companies are switching to renewable energy sources.
questions.explanation_zh: 文章主要讲述大型化石燃料公司通过增加塑料生产来维持盈利和延续业务。

questions.question: How does the speaker feel about the industry's plans?

questions.explanation_zh: 说话者对行业增加塑料生产的计划感到震惊和担忧，这也是她写书的动机。

clips.title: 塑料如何战后走进生活
clips.info_takeaway: 二战后塑料产业有意通过广告和营销将塑料产品推入人们生活，形成长期消费习惯。

source.episode: Who's to blame for all this plastic?

lines.en: Plastics appeal was obvious from the start.
lines.zh: 塑料的吸引力一开始就很明显。

lines.en: It could be molded into different shapes, given texture, made in different colors.
lines.zh: 它可以塑造成各种形状，加上纹理，还能做成不同颜色。

lines.en: Celluloid, a plastic patented in 1869, was used to make billiard balls, combs and eventually
lines.zh: 赛璐珞是一种1869年申请专利的塑料，用来做台球、梳子，后来还用来

lines.en: film for movies.
lines.zh: 制作电影胶片。

lines.en: Then along came newer plastics like plexiglass, nylon, polyethylene, all derived from the
lines.zh: 后来又出现了更新的塑料，比如有机玻璃、尼龙、聚乙烯，它们都来自

lines.en: byproducts of fossil fuels, each with distinctive characteristics.
lines.zh: 化石燃料的副产品，各有不同的特性。

lines.en: Now, polyethylene coated cables, as I learned in Beth Gardner's new book, Plastic Ink,
lines.zh: 我在贝丝·加德纳的新书《塑料墨水》里了解到，聚乙烯包覆的电缆

lines.en: improved radar so much during World War II that it helped turn the naval side of the
lines.zh: 在二战期间大幅提升了雷达性能，帮助扭转了海战的

lines.en: conflict in favor of the Allies.
lines.zh: 局势，让盟军占了上风。

lines.en: But as the war wound down, manufacturers had a decision to make.
lines.zh: 但随着战争接近尾声，制造商得做出决定。

lines.en: What would they do with all the plastic now?
lines.zh: 他们现在会怎么处理这些塑料？

lines.en: Here's Beth.
lines.zh: 贝丝来了。

lines.en: After the war or as the war was winding down, even before it ended, you see the industry
lines.zh: 在战争结束或快结束的时候，甚至还没结束，你就能看到这个行业

lines.en: kind of gaining this awareness that, number one, they're going to be able to ramp up
lines.zh: 开始意识到第一，他们可以加快生产

lines.en: production as they shift towards a peacetime economy.
lines.zh: 因为经济要转向和平时期。

lines.en: And number two, who were they going to sell all this plastic to?
lines.zh: 第二，他们要把这些塑料卖给谁？

lines.en: And I think what was so shocking to me as I researched this book was the the
lines.zh: 我在写这本书时最让我震惊的是

lines.en: deliberateness and intention with which the industry pushed plastic into our lives.
lines.zh: 这个行业是多么有计划、有目的地把塑料推到我们生活里。

lines.en: So it really sort of intersects with the world of marketing and advertising.
lines.zh: 这跟营销和广告的世界紧密相连。

lines.en: Yeah, you write about how suddenly after the war, there's all this marketing for
lines.zh: 是啊，你写到战争结束后突然出现大量的营销

lines.en: plastic toys.
lines.zh: 塑料玩具

questions.question: What's the main point of the passage?

options: A. Surprised by how intentionally plastics were promoted to the public.
B. Angry that plastics replaced traditional materials.

D. Indifferent to the marketing strategies used.
questions.explanation_zh: 说话者对行业在战后刻意推广塑料的行为感到震惊。

clips.title: 月球背面失联前的告别
clips.info_takeaway: 阿耳忒弥斯II宇航员在月球背面与地球失联40分钟前，向地球传递爱与使命感。
